coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of environmentally-friendly carbon quantum dots from orange juice for selective detection of Fe3+ ions, antibacterial activity, and bio-imaging applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kasirajan, Kasinathan
Karunakaran, Marimuthu
Choi, Hong Kyoon
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a unique fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, have sparked widespread interest. They are highly biocompatible, water-soluble, chemically stable, and non-toxic. In this investigation, green-emissive CQDs were synthesized using orange juice as a precursor in a simple hydrothermal approach. The physicochemical investigation supports the particle size and functional groups of CQDs. The synthesized CQDs have emitted the bright green fluorescence light under the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. They have exhibited good stability, high water solubility, and excitation-dependent characteristics. Moreover, the water soluble CQDs have excellent fluorescence quenching for Fe3+ ions, as well as outstanding selectivity and sensitivity. The biocompatibility test was carried out on MCF-7 cells, and the produced material demonstrated superior biocompatibility at 100 μg/mL concentrations. The antibacterial activity was tested against four different bacteria. The S. aureus and K. pneumoniae bacteria have the best antibacterial activity compared to others. The antibacterial mechanism found that CQDs with abundant −NH3 + groups on their surface exhibit greater antibacterial activity. Also, it’s successfully prevents bacterial biofilms caused by E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus and B. subtilis. Moreover, the CQDs have been proven to be a nontoxic probe for multicolor imaging of MCF-7 cells. These findings indicate that the produced CQDs are effective multicolor fluorescent probes for MCF-7 cells and offer a new outlook for diagnostic tools in the biomedical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Orange juice
Fe3+ ion sensing
Antibacterial activity
MCF-7 cell

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program (NRF-2022R1I1A3072815) through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of CQDs, (b) FTIR spectrum of orange extract and CQDs, (c), UV–visible spectrum of CQDs (Inset shows under day light (left) and UV-light (right), and (d) Raman spectra of CQDs.
(a−c) TEM images of CQDs with different magnifications.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CQDs, (b−c) High-resolution spectrum of C1s, N1s, and O1s.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelength (interval 20 nm) and (b) Excitation dependent maximum emission wavelengths.

(a) pH dependent fluorescence emission spectra, (b) Fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F0) of CQDs with different metal ions, (c) Fluorescence intensity changes with Fe3+ concentrations from 10 to 500 μM, and (d) Calibration plot of F/F0 vs. Fe3+ ion concentration (inset image shows 10–100 μM concentrations).
FL quenching mechanism of CQDs mixed with Fe3+ ions.
(a, b) Optical microscopy images of control and treatments of CQDs, (c) Cell viability values of CQDs at different concentrations against MCF-7 cells.

(a) Typical images of E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and B. subtilis after treatment with different concentrations of CQDs (b) Bacterial viability values of E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and B. subtilis at different CQD concentrations.

(a) Confocal laser microscopic imagining of control, E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and B. subtilis with different concentrations (25 μg/mL-200 μg/mL), (b) Biofilm inhibition (%) analysis of CQDs against E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and B. subtilis bacteria.
Confocal microscopy images of CQDs treated with MCF-7 cells; bright-field, blue, green, red, and merged images with 405 nm, 488 nm and 561 nm excitation wavelengths.
Schematic representation of the synthesis of orange juice derived CQDs.
FL CQDs from various carbon sources for Fe3+ ion detection.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of environmentally-friendly carbon quantum dots from orange juice for selective detection of Fe3+ ions, antibacterial activity, and bio-imaging applications

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Advanced Materials Engineering, Kongju National University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a unique fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, have sparked widespread interest. They are highly biocompatible, water-soluble, chemically stable, and non-toxic. In this investigation, green-emissive CQDs were synthesized using orange juice as a precursor in a simple hydrothermal approach. The physicochemical investigation supports the particle size and functional groups of CQDs. The synthesized CQDs have emitted the bright green fluorescence light under the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. They have exhibited good stability, high water solubility, and excitation-dependent characteristics. Moreover, the water soluble CQDs have excellent fluorescence quenching for Fe3+ ions, as well as outstanding selectivity and sensitivity. The biocompatibility test was carried out on MCF-7 cells, and the produced material demonstrated superior biocompatibility at 100 μg/mL concentrations. The antibacterial activity was tested against four different bacteria. The S. aureus and K. pneumoniae bacteria have the best antibacterial activity compared to others. The antibacterial mechanism found that CQDs with abundant −NH3 + groups on their surface exhibit greater antibacterial activity. Also, it’s successfully prevents bacterial biofilms caused by E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus and B. subtilis. Moreover, the CQDs have been proven to be a nontoxic probe for multicolor imaging of MCF-7 cells. These findings indicate that the produced CQDs are effective multicolor fluorescent probes for MCF-7 cells and offer a new outlook for diagnostic tools in the biomedical field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel form of carbon-based material that has received substantial attention because of their great photostability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, incredible water solubility, high sensitivity, and selectivity for target analytes. CQDs are a novel form of nanoparticle that has gained appeal since its unexpected discovery in 2004 [1–3]. The CQDs are smaller than 10 nm in size and have a discrete quasi-spherical amorphous character, which occurs as abundant surface functional groups. Generally, CQD contain O/N−containing functional groups (e.g., amino (–NH2), carboxyl (–COOH), and hydroxyl (–OH) on their surfaces. These functional groups on the CQD surface can be effectively employed as chemical and biological sensing devices [4,5]. CQDs have been reported for a variety of applications, including cell labeling [6], drug distribution [7], metal ion detection [8], fluorescent inks [9], optical imaging [10], bio-sensing [11], photocatalysis [12], and energy storage [13]. Furthermore, a variety of strategies for synthesizing CQDs have been reported, including "bottom−up" and "top−down" approaches. The bottom-up strategy is known for its expensive and reliance on specialized instrumentation. Alternatively, the top-down technique offers simplicity and cost effectiveness, with various techniques such as (i) chemical ablation, (ii) electrochemical carbonization, (iii) laser ablation, (iv) microwave irradiation, and (v) hydrothermal/solvothermal methods being explored [14-18]. Among these strategies, we selected a hydrothermal method for the synthesis of CQDs. The hydrothermal process is recognized to be low-cost, environmentally friendly, controls particle size, and has a high level of purity. Researchers have reported a wide variety of bio-sources that may be used in hydrothermal procedures to create fluorescent CQDs. These include papaya juice, rice husks, sugarcane baggage, coffee grounds, watermelon peels, lemon, banana peels, and pineapple [19–21].

The development of highly selective and sensitive probes for measuring heavy metal ions is an active research area in the fields of chemistry, environmental science, and biology. In this scenario, the rising human population and industrial growth lead to the discharge of huge quantities of heavy metals [22,23]. It has a tremendous influence on both environmental and human health. Ferric ion (Fe3+) is a vital transition metal in living beings and the most prevalent metal cation in nature. Fe3+ is engaged in an enormous number of biological functions, including catalysis, the transfer of electrons, oxygen distribution, transport, and enzyme activity at the cell level. However, increasing Fe3+ ions in drinking water has become a serious hazard to human health. The increasing level of Fe3+ in the human body generates multiple health difficulties, such as cancer of various organs (heart, pancreas, and liver), Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson’s syndrome [24–26]. Moreover, the abundance of iron in the environment and drinking water is significantly higher than the other heavy metal ions, such as lead, zinc, magnesium, etc. Therefore, accurate detection of Fe3+ in drinking water is essential. The methodologies for identifying Fe3+ ions use numerous methods (i.e., atomic absorption spectrometry, coupled plasma mass spectrometry, electron paramagnetic resonance, electrochemistry, and colorimetric analysis) [27,28]. However, these approaches require sophisticated machinery, difficult sample preparation procedures, time-consuming processes, high expenses, and skilled analyzers. Currently, CQD-based fluorescence assay sensors are recognized as excellent instruments for identifying heavy metals, with the objectives of high sensitivity, selectivity, fewer interferences, cost-effectiveness, and quick response [29].

CQD also possess notable antibacterial properties, which are attributed to their unique surface chemistry. A recent analysis demonstrates that the bactericidal efficacy of the CQDs is linked to their surface charge; both positively and negatively surface-charged CQDs exhibit bactericidal effects, while uncharged CQDs remain inactive [30–32] Huang et al. reported that super-cationic CQDs possess strong antibacterial activities against multidrug-resistant bacteria and superior efficacy in treating eye-related bacterial infections [33]. Peili Li et al. found that polyamine-functionalized CQDs, with antibacterial and antibiofilm activity dependent on their surface chemistry, are particularly effective against E. coli bacteria [34]. Hence, positively charged CQDs are particularly effective in inhibiting bacterial growth, underscoring the necessity of controlling surface charge during CQD fabrication. This paper highlights the ability of N-containing CQDs to effectively bind the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane, significantly enhancing their bactericidal action. Furthermore, the CQD surface may have the capacity to initiate the activation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which also contribute to their antibacterial properties.

Another fascinating application of CQDs is bioimaging. They contain distinct properties like a tunable color, low toxicity, good photostability, and high biocompatibility. Bioimaging is a tool for tracking biological processes and analyzing cell shape and physiological functions. Cancer represents a significant threat to human health, leading to extensive efforts in biomedical research aimed at developing innovative methods for early cancer diagnosis, and treatment. As part of efforts, researchers have investigated the bioimaging features of biomass−derived CQDs in a variety of cancer cells, including "HepG2, K-562, MCF-7, Hep3B, etc."[35–37] A few fascinating aspects of CQDs for evaluating bio-imaging activities in biological systems are as follows: (i) CQDs used in bioimaging have some basic characteristics, including small size (2–10 nm), water solubility, high quantum yield, tunable emission wavelengths, and excellent photostability, (ii) CQDs are chemically inert, making them ideal for both in vitro and in vivo imaging, (iii) CQDs are feasible for surface modification. They are readily coated with a variety of bioactive compounds, (iv) Finally, CQDs provide low cost, excellent stability, and biocompatibility.

In our current work, we suggest an environmentally friendly and one-step approach to synthesizing CQDs using a hydrothermal reaction utilizing orange juice. The presence of abundant surface functional groups in as−synthesized CQDs was verified by FT-IR. In addition, XRD, UV-Vis, Raman, HR-TEM, and XPS were employed for comprehensive characterization of the CQDs. The synthesized CQDs exhibited high selectivity towards Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, the CQDs demonstrated outstanding antibacterial efficacy against several pathogens as well as good biocompatibility during cellular imaging. Our finding emphasizes the potential of these CQDs as a simple, efficient, sensitive, and selective tool for bioimaging and metal ion detection.

The aqueous form of ammonia (25 %) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich, Rep. of Korea. Deionized (DI) water was employed throughout this investigation. The orange was bought at a local market in Cheonan, Rep. of Korea. The 500 g of juicy oranges were properly cleaned with DI water and sliced into tiny slices without seeds. The blender was employed to completely grind the sliced fruit. The crushed orange was filtered through Whatman filter paper and then combined with a small amount of DI water. Finally, the orange extract was gathered and kept in the refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from orange juice
ce:section.ce:para: The orange juice-derived CQDs synthesis protocols are presented in Scheme 1. The highly fluorescent CQDs were produced using orange juice via a hydrothermal approach. In the beginning, 50 mL of orange extract was put in a Teflon−lined stainless steel autoclave with a 100 mL inner capacity. Additionally, 2 mL of aqueous ammonia solution (25 %) was added to the orange extract. Following that, the autoclave was closed tightly and stored at a temperature of 180 °C in the oven for 12 h. The autoclave was allowed to cool naturally and we got a brownish solution. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min and passed through a 0.22 μm filter. The resultant CQDs were kept in a refrigerator at 5 °C for future usage.

The synthesized N-CQDs were characterized by various physicochemical techniques. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out using a PANalytical X’Pert3 MRD diffractometer with monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54 Å) at 40 kV and 30 mA and were recorded in the range from 10 to 80° (2θ). The FTIR spectra of (Bruker, Model: ALPHA II) CQDs at a frequency range of 4000–400 cm−1 were used to characterize the functional group of these samples. The UV–visible absorbance spectra were recorded from 200 to 800 nm using a Thermo Scientific, Evolution 201 spectrophotometer. Raman spectrum was recorded on a Thermo Scientific DXR Smart Raman spectrometer with the range from 50 to 3500 cm−1. The morphology and size of the prepared CQDs are characterized by High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM JEOL-2200). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were achieved using a K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific). Photoluminescence spectra were obtained on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (SCINCO, FluoroMate FS-2) at room temperature.

The fluorescence emission of the synthesized CQDs was measured at excitation wavelength of 420 nm and strong emission wavelength at 515 nm. The strong fluorescence emission of the CQDs decreased upon addition of different metal ions. There is no change in the fluorescence emission of CQDs before addition of metal ions. The detection of Fe3+ ions was performed using 1 mg mL−1 of CQDs and the addition of standard Fe3+ ions in different concentrations. The sensitivity and selectivity of CQDs in sensing different metal ions (Al3+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, and Cu2+) was measured by mixing the aqueous metal ions stock solution. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs without the addition of any metal ions was measured and set as F0 (blank/control). The synthesized CQDs showed high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ ions than other metal ions by quenching the fluorescence emission of the CQDs. The synthesized CQDs highly sensed Fe3+ metal ions and further the detection was carried out at lower concentration to determine the limit of detection.

The antimicrobial activity of the CQDs was tested by the measurement of OD600 and the colony count method. The bacteria solutions were diluted by a factor of 10 with PBS containing a series of known CQDs and seeded into 96-well plates. The plate was then placed in a shaking incubator (180 rpm) at 30 °C for 24 h, and the OD600 values were measured at different times by a microplate reader. Finally, the number of bacterial colonies was calculated according to the following formula:
Viability ( V ) =
OD of treated sample
OD of control sample
× 100

The monolayer cell culture was trypsinized and the cell count was adjusted to 1.0 ×105 cells/mL using respective media containing 10 % FBS. To each well of the 96 well microtiter plate, 100 µl of the diluted cell suspension (50,000cells/well) was added. After 24 h, when a partial monolayer was formed, the supernatant was flicked off, washed the monolayer once with medium and 100 µl of different test concentrations of test drugs were added on to the partial monolayer in microtiter plates. The plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 24hrs in 5 % CO2 atmosphere. After incubation the test solutions in the wells were discarded and 100 µl of MTT (5 mg/10 mL of MTT in PBS) was added to each well. The plates were incubated for 4 h at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 atmosphere. The supernatant was removed and 100 µl of DMSO was added and the plates were gently shaken to solubilize the formed formazan. The absorbance was measured using a microplate reader at a wavelength of 590 nm. The percentage growth inhibition was calculated using the following formula and concentration of test drug needed to inhibit cell growth by 50 % (IC50) values is generated from the dose-response curves for each cell line.
% Cell viability =
OD of treated sample
OD of control sample
× 100

The XRD was utilized to detect the crystalline or amorphous form of the synthesized CQDs. The XRD spectra of CQDs ( Fig. 1a) displayed two distinct peaks at 2θ = (21.3° and 43.5°) that were ascribed to the (002) and (100) diffraction patterns of graphitic carbon. This finding reveals that synthesized CQDs were graphitic structure, which also agrees well with the earlier published papers [38,39]. FTIR analysis was performed to investigate the functional groups on the CQD surface. Fig. 1b displays the FTIR spectra of orange juice and CQDs. The wide peak that emerged at 3480 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of O−H/N−H. The important peaks presented at 1650, 1557, and 1245 cm−1 correlated to CO, C−N, and C−O−C, respectively. This data supports the presence of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine functional groups on the surface [40,41]. The optical characteristics of CQDs were examined by UV−Vis spectrum as shown in Fig. 1c. The band seen at 240 nm is ascribed to π→π* transitions of the CC bonds in sp2 carbon regions. The second band seen at a wavelength of 334 nm can be attributed to the n→π* transition of both CO and C−N/CN bonds [42]. The inset shot in Fig. 1(c) shows the aqueous solution of CQDs under visible and UV light. Fig. 1d displays the Raman spectra of CQDs. The peak seen at 1351 cm−1 is identified with defects and disordered graphite structure, denoted as the D band. Another peak observed at 1597 cm−1 belongs to the G band, which is characteristic of the sp2 carbon graphitic structure [43]. The ID/IG ratio was determined to be 0.85, indicating the partial amorphous characteristic of the CQDs.

The size and morphology of the prepared CQDs were investigated by high-resolution transmission electron micrographic (HR−TEM) studies. The HR-TEM images of CQDs at different magnifications are provided in Fig. 2(a–c). The CQDs have a regular spherical shape with averages ranging from 2–6 nm. The observed d-spacing value is about 0.21 nm, related to the (100) plane of the hexagonal graphite structure.

XPS is an effective method for analyzing the structure and chemical composition of materials. The XPS spectra of survey and individual C1s, N1s, and O1s spectrum are displayed in Fig. 3(a−d). The XPS spectrum exhibits the three major peaks at C1s (284.7), N1s (400.3), and O1s (537.9) are shown in Fig. 3a. The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) exhibits the three major peaks appeared at 284.2 eV, 285.6 eV, and 286.5 eV related to the C−C/CC, C−N, and C−O bonds. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) indicates that there were two peaks at 399.5 eV, and 400.1 eV are linked to the C−N−C, and N−H bonds. The O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3d) depicts binding energies of 532.1 and 533.2 eV for the C−O−C and C−OH bands, respectively [44–48]. These observations suggest that the synthesized materials are nitrogen−containing CQDs with numerous surface bonds.

The optical characteristics of CQDs were investigated via fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy. CQDs are distinguished by their size-dependent fluorescence activity and emission wavelength. It is widely established that the surface condition of CQDs is intimately related to their fluorescence behavior. Thus, fluorescence emission of CQDs was investigated under varying process parameters. The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs in aqueous solution at various excitation wavelengths are presented in Fig. 4a. The highest emission intensity is observed at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm, resulting an emission around 515 nm, which provides a clear explanation for the green emission observed in the CQD solution depicted in Scheme 1. The quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was investigated at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, and the yield was approximately 4.1 %. The fluorescence emission intensity increases as the excitation wavelength increases from 300 nm − 420 nm. However, emission intensity decreases with further excitation wavelength increases (440 nm − 520 nm). Fig. 4b shows the excitation-dependent maximum emission wavelength. The maximum emission wavelength increased significantly from 455 to 582 nm while the excitation wavelength increase from 300 nm to 520 nm, which could be attributable to the variable particle sizes or surface energy traps of the CQDs.

The fluorescence response of CQDs was investigated in various pH conditions (pH 2–12) and the results are provided in Fig. 5a. The CQDs exhibit FL intensity within a defined range across the pH range of 2−9. However, FL intensity decreases rapidly at pH values >9. This phenomenon can be attributed to the intense intermolecular hydrogen bonding that occurs between surface functional groups at extreme pH environments. Therefore, the synthesized CQDs are ideal candidates for ion detection under pH 9 conditions. Moreover, selectivity is an important metric to consider for the sensor application. To evaluate this, changes in fluorescence intensity in the presence of various metal ions were investigated. The selectivity of CQD towards various metal ions was investigated using fluorescence spectra with the addition of Al3+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, and Cu2+, as shown in Fig. S1. Selectivity testing was conducted using various metal ions at 500 µM concentrations, with experiments carried out at room temperature. The FL intensities of CQDs with and without metal ions are denoted by F and F0. The bar graph was drawn by plotting F/F0 versus various metal ions (Fig. 5b). The results reveal a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity in the presence of Fe3+, while other metal ions show minimal or no interference with fluorescence. This finding indicates capability of our synthesized fluorescent CQDs for detecting Fe3+ ions. Sensitivity test was conducted to assess the response of the FL signals before and after exposure to varying concentration of Fe3+ ions ranging from 0 to 500 μM (Fig. 5c). The outcomes suggest that the fluorescence intensity of the CQD quenches proportionally as the concentration of Fe3+ ions increases. The linear relationship between Fe3+ concentrations versus fluorescence intensities (F/F0) of CQDs was fitted, as illustrated in Fig. 5d. Notably the F/F0 plot shows a solid linear connection within the low concentrations range of 0–100 μM, with a regression value of approximately 0.996. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated as 0.7 μM using Eq. (1).
(1)
LOD = 3 ×
σ
S
where, σ is the standard deviation and s is the slope of the straight line. The comparison of CQDs from various carbon precursors for Fe3+ ions detection is displayed in Table 1. This result exhibits the (LOD) value is 0.7 μM, which is comparable to previously published values [40, 49–55].

The metal ion sensing methods are currently under scrutiny due to the complexity of the factors involved. The FL quenching mechanism is one of the most promising techniques for detecting metal ions [56,57]. The FL quenching mechanism of CQDs in a mixture of Fe3+ ions is depicted in Fig. 6. The surface of most CQDs is covered with functional groups such as carboxyl, amine, and hydroxyl groups, promoting strong interaction to metal ions. Consequently, energy transfer between CQDs and metal ions could lead to quenching or decreases in emission spectra. In particular, adding Fe3+ ions to the produced CQD water solution drastically reduced the fluorescence emission intensity. One possible explanation for this significant quenching action is the generation of CQD- Fe3+ complexes. Electrostatic interaction between Fe3+ ions and CQDs functional groups is the primary recognition mechanism reported in CQDs based system for Fe3+ detection [58]. The strong quenching of fluorescence intensity was caused by Fe3+ ions, which have a stronger affinity for nitrogen and oxygen on their surface. Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that the non-radiative electron transfer process between CQDs and Fe3+ is responsible for the fluorescence quenching. The oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl play an important role in the water solubility and strong attraction to metal ions, enabling highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions.

Biocompatibility is one of the critical characteristics that determine the feasibility of successfully delivering CQDs for theranostics applications. The optical microscopy images of as-prepared CQDs against MCF-7 cells were investigated using an MTT test as displayed in Fig. 7(a, b) as well as along with the cell viability values of CQDs at different dosages (0 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL) displayed in Fig. 7c. As the quantity of CQDs increase, there is a slight decline in cell viability. However, within the range of 10 μg/mL and 50 μg/mL of CQDs, the cell viability remains consistently above 90 %. Even at concentrations as high 100 μg/mL, approximately 81 % of the MCF-7 cells remained viable. The results thus imply that CQDs have good biocompatibility with MCF-7 cells indicating their non-cytotoxic nature and suitability for biomedical applications.

The antibacterial activity of the synthesized CQDs was investigated against four different bacteria: Gram-positive (B. subtilis, S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli, K. pneumoniae). The standard plate counting method was used to determine the survival of bacteria treated with CQDs for 24 h. Fig. 8a demonstrates the correlation between bacterial viability and CQD concentrations of 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, and 200 μg/mL. The bar graph in Fig. 8b illustrates a clear decrease in bacterial viability as CQD concentration rise. The extent of viability reduction varies among different bacterial strains; however, at a CQD concentration reaches 200 μg/mL, viability drops below 5 % for all tested bacteria. These findings demonstrate that CQD have effective broad spectrum antibacterial activity. The difference in viability reduction among bacterial strain can be attributed to their distinct cell wall structures. For instance, S. aureus has a thick membrane (20–80 nm) containing sugars and amino acids, whereas E. coli has thin (10 nm) layers of protein and lipids. These structural variations influence how bacteria respond to treatments such as CQDs.

The antibacterial mechanism of CQDs is a complex and diverse approach from that of traditional antibiotics, which includes reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation, deterioration of cell structure, and cytoplasm leakage. In general, an antibiotic interacts with bacteria by electrostatic action and chemical conjugations. The possible antibacterial mechanism of CQDs is depicted in Fig. S2. According to the previous report, the antibacterial mechanism of CQDs is usually assumed to be based on electrostatic interactions and ROS production. ROS production has a significant impact on antibacterial activity [59,60]. The synthesized CQDs have a positive charge on their surface due to the appearance of hydroxyl and nitrogen groups, as evidenced by FTIR and XPS spectra. Additionally, zeta potential analysis (Fig.S3) revealed the positively charged (+8.5 mV). This positive charge implies that CQDs likely bind to the bacteria via electrostatic interaction, resulting in the disrupting of bacterial cell walls. The antibacterial mechanism of CQDs as follows as: (i) in first positively charged surface of CQDs can easily attached with negatively charged cell membrane, (ii) penetrate the cell wall and produced the ROS, (iii) ROS has damaged to the bacterial cell, (iv) finally, kill the bacteria. Moreover, several investigations have revealed that nitrogen-containing CQDs have strong antibacterial efficacy against Gram +ve and Gram -ve pathogens. The above result suggests that the synthesized CQDs have potentially inhibited and killed the bacteria.

Microorganisms develop surface-adherent communities that produce a sturdy coating of a main polysaccharide matrix called biofilms, which perform a vital part in bacterial infection and proliferation. The polysaccharide matrix contains proteins, polysaccharides, lipids, and DNA. These biofilms possess significant resistance to antibiotics and can impair the host immune system. The antibiofilm efficacy of CQDs was investigated across concentrations (25, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) in bacterial culture. The biofilm confocal microscopy images of CQDs with different concentrations (25 μg/mL-200 μg/mL) are shown in Fig. 9a. After 24 h of incubation, the treated group of the biofilm exhibited a significantly reduced cells count compared to the control groups (Fig. 9a). Notably, treatment with 200 μg/mL CQDs inhibit 82 % of E. coli, 91 % of K. pneumoniae, 89 % of S. aureus, and 71 % of B. subtilis (Fig. 9b). Importantly, the structure of the biofilm after 200 μg/mL treatment is almost destroyed in the K. pneumonia, and S. aureus groups. These finding indicate that CQDs not only prevent biofilm formation but also effectively reduce the microcolony development.

The MTT experiment demonstrated that biocompatibility of the produced CQDs which have further been utilized in vitro bioimaging studies. The CQD has been introduced into the breast cancer MCF-7 cell line and examined in vitro using confocal laser scanning microscopy. Tunable fluorescence and a size of less than 10 nm can be used in cell labeling. Fig. 10 displays bright field, fluorescence, and merged images of MCF-7 cells. Positively charged CQDs are easily attracted to the negatively charged MCF-7 cell membrane. When MCF-7 cells were observed under a microscope using various excitation wavelengths, they exhibited effective blue, green, and red emissions. The intracellular portions of CQD-treated MCF-7 cells showed intense green fluorescence, indicating CQDs penetration into the cell membrane without causing morphological damage, confirming their low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility. These results highlight the multifunctionality of synthesized CQDs as biomedical nanoagents, promising for future clinical applications.

In summary, we have described a facile, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly method for producing water-soluble CQDs using orange juice as a carbon source. This technology has several benefits over other synthetic processes. The produced CQDs exhibit good stability, high water solubility, and excitation-dependent characteristics. Physicochemical characterization of the CQDs confirmed the presence of carboxylic, amine, and hydroxyl functional groups on spherical surfaces of the CQDs, with average particle sizes ranging from 2–6 nm. The FL spectrum of CQDs exhibits the excellent photostability and excitation-dependent behavior, with potential for efficient fluorescence quenching capability toward Fe3+ ions, along with outstanding selectivity and sensitivity in aqueous solutions. Furthermore, our findings highlight the potential biological applications of these CQDs. The biocompatibility of the CQDs has ∼ 81 % of cell viability at 100 μg/mL concentrations. The antibacterial assay against four different bacteria reveals significant activity against S. aureus and K. pneumoniae. Moreover, the CQDs have been proven to be a nontoxic probe for multicolor imaging of MCF-7 cells. These findings reveal the versatility and utility of the proposed CQDs in sensor and pharmacy applications.

Hong Kyoon Choi:Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Kasirajan Kasinathan:Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Karunakaran Marimuthu:Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program (NRF-2022R1I1A3072815) through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2024.113535.
